name: Iraq
tactical_document:
# Iraq — Tactical Profile

## Identity & Philosophy
Iraq under **Graham Arnold** (the former Australia head coach hired to professionalise the side, with Rene Meulensteen as assistant) is **direct, physical, and ruthlessly organized** — a team that has finally ended a 40-year World Cup absence, qualifying through the AFC route (UAE in the Asian play-off, Bolivia in the intercontinental play-off) and into Group I alongside France, Senegal and Norway. Arnold's identity is **Anglo-Saxon work-rate + Iraqi technical flair**: a compact mid-block, hard selective pressing, vertical transitions, and intelligent set-piece routines. The final 26 blends an experienced, set-piece-savvy spine with a European-based contingent (Ali Jasim at Como, Al-Hamadi at Luton Town on loan from Ipswich, Al-Ammari at Cracovia, Qasem and Iqbal in the Eredivisie). Captain Jalal Hassan holds the gloves. Iraq is the **physical bully** of the AFC region — they will out-run, out-jump, and out-foul most opponents while remaining tactically disciplined, but they are clearly the lowest-rated side in a brutal group. Arnold has installed a level of professional structure the team has lacked for years.

**Matchday 1 (16 June, vs Norway — lost 1-4):** Iraq were beaten 1-4 as Erling Haaland scored a brace; Iraq's only goal came before Aymen Hussein later turned a ball into his own net for Norway's fourth. The starting XI was a flat **4-4-2**, with Zidane Iqbal surprisingly on the bench (Arnold preferred the more physical Zaid Ismael) and Akam Hashim at CB ahead of Rebin Sulaka. **Aymen Hussein was withdrawn at half-time with a knock.**

**Matchday 2 (22 June, vs France — lost 0-3, Philadelphia):** France's Maignan-marshalled side won 3-0. Arnold kept the compact 4-4-2 and again left Iqbal on the bench, but made one notable change in the wide-midfield band: **Ahmed Qasem started at right midfield** (in place of the knocked Ali Jasim), with Bayesh on the left, Al-Ammari and Zaid Ismael central, and Al-Hamadi / Aymen Hussein up top. Iraq defended deep, conceded few clear-cut chances early, but were undone by France's quality.

**Matchday 3 outlook (26 June, vs Senegal, BMO Field, Toronto):** Iraq are **bottom of Group I on 0 points (GD -6) and already mathematically eliminated** — but so are Senegal pointless, making this a pride-and-redemption dead rubber between two winless sides. The biggest team-news question is up top: **Aymen Hussein (the HT withdrawal vs Norway) is a major doubt**; if he cannot start, Al-Hamadi leads the line with a partner. This profile keeps Hussein in the XI as the talisman while flagging his fitness, and retains Ahmed Qasem at RM from the France start.

## Formation
- Shape: **4-4-2** (two flat banks of four out of possession; collapses to **4-5-1** against superior opposition with Al-Hamadi dropping into midfield).
- Role mapping (roster order in `iraq.yaml`):
  - index 0: GK — **Jalal Hassan** (#12, captain) — traditional shot-stopper, less of a sweeper; commands his box with the back four sitting deep in front. (Ahmed Basil / Fahad Talib are rotation options.)
  - index 1: LB — **Merchas Doski** (#23) — most attacking of the back four, the natural width-giver on the left; overlapping fullback.
  - index 2: LCB — **Akam Hashim** (#5, Al-Zawraa) — physical and aerially strong (strength 15); reads danger and clears.
  - index 3: RCB — **Zaid Tahseen** (#4) — physical partner (strength 15), wins headers in both boxes; the defensive organiser.
  - index 4: RB — **Hussein Ali** (#3) — disciplined fullback, defensive-first, rarely overlaps deep.
  - index 5: LM — **Ibrahim Bayesh** (#8) — the chief creator from the left, cuts inside, late runs into the box, set-piece taker.
  - index 6: LCM — **Amir Al-Ammari** (#16) — the destroyer, the screen, the tactical fouler.
  - index 7: RCM — **Zaid Ismael** (#6, Al-Talaba) — the MD1/MD2 starter ahead of Iqbal; a hard-running, defensively diligent central midfielder who shields the back four and recycles simply. (Zidane Iqbal is the creative sub who can take this slot when Iraq chases a game.)
  - index 8: RM — **Ahmed Qasem** (#11) — the MD2 starter at right midfield; quick, direct wide threat (speed 15, dribbling 14) who carries in transition. (Ali Jasim is the alternative on this flank.)
  - index 9: ST — **Aymen Hussein** (#18) — the target forward, hold-up player, aerial threat. Strength 15, shoot 15. The team's leading scorer — **a fitness doubt for this match after his Norway knock.**
  - index 10: ST — **Ali Al-Hamadi** (#9, Luton Town, on loan from Ipswich) — energetic striker, runs the channels off Aymen Hussein, leads the press, and drops into midfield in the 4-5-1; expected to lead the line if Hussein is unfit.

## Style of Play

### Build-up
Direct. Iraq builds from the back when uncontested but goes long the moment any press arrives. The default long ball is aimed at Aymen Hussein for a knock-down to Al-Hamadi running off him or Bayesh / Qasem arriving from wide. Iraq does NOT prioritize possession — they prioritize getting the ball forward and getting their shape back. Against a winless Senegal who must chase the win, Iraq may see slightly more of the ball than in their first two games and can be a touch braver in transition.

### Pressing
**Mid-block, hard-running, trigger-based.** Iraq drops to the halfway line and waits. Triggers: opposition GK takes a heavy touch, back-pass under duress, opposition midfielder receives on the half-turn. Aymen Hussein and Al-Hamadi lead the press as a front two with curving runs; Bayesh and Qasem jump from the wings; Al-Ammari is the central duel-winner. The press is hard but selective — Iraq's stamina ratings (mostly 14-15) can't sustain a 90-minute high press.

### Defensive shape
Compact **4-4-2** dropping to **4-5-1**: Bayesh and Qasem tuck into a flat midfield line alongside Al-Ammari and Zaid Ismael, while Al-Hamadi drops onto the opposition pivot and Aymen Hussein screens the deepest CB. The back four sits 22-25 units off goal and stays narrow to protect the box. Against Senegal's pace (Ismaila Sarr, Sadio Mane) Iraq must guard the channels and avoid getting stretched in behind.

### Wide play
**Asymmetric.** Left side: Bayesh cuts inside, Doski overlaps for the natural width. Right side: Qasem carries and drives inside, Hussein Ali stays deep as cover (no overlap). The crosses come from the left (Doski) and from deep set-pieces. Iraq is a **crossing team** when they get behind the opposition fullback — Aymen Hussein attacks the near post.

### Final third
Patterns: long ball to Aymen Hussein's chest, knock-down to Al-Hamadi's run or Bayesh's late arrival, shot from 18 yards. Doski's left-flank cross to Aymen Hussein at the near post. Qasem's carry-and-slip to Al-Hamadi sprinting in behind. With both sides desperate for a first point, Iraq's best hope is to be clinical with their few chances and lethal from set-pieces.

## Set Pieces
**Iraq is a set-piece-dangerous side** with Hashim and Tahseen as aerial threats in both boxes — likely their single most realistic route to a goal.
- Attacking corners: **Bayesh** in-swingers from the right (left foot), out-swingers from the left. Targets: Tahseen (penalty spot), Hashim (back post), Aymen Hussein (near post flick-on).
- Defending corners: man-marking heavy. Tahseen marks the most dangerous opposition striker; Hashim marks the second; zonal markers fill the six-yard box; Jalal Hassan stays on the line.
- Free kicks: **Bayesh** direct from the right half-space and from any angle within 27 yards; Zidane Iqbal takes over central dead-balls when he is on.
- Penalties: **Aymen Hussein** primary, **Bayesh** secondary, **Qasem** tertiary (Iqbal an option when on).

## decide() Decision Priorities
1. **If my role == "GK" (index 0, Jalal Hassan, #12) and the team has a goal-kick:** Default to a long ball aimed at the index-9 Aymen Hussein, unless no opposition press is detected.
2. **If my player_id ends with "_9" (ST Aymen Hussein, #18) and a long ball is incoming:** Win the aerial duel — strength 15. Knock down to the index-10 Al-Hamadi or the index-5 Bayesh arriving from the left.
3. **If my player_id ends with "_8" (RM Ahmed Qasem, #11) and I have the ball on the right flank:** Carry forward (speed 15, dribbling 14); cut inside onto my stronger foot; Shoot if angle opens within 22 units, else slip the index-10 Al-Hamadi's channel-run.
4. **If team_phase == "defending" and the opposition is past midfield:** Drop into the **4-4-2** banks at the halfway line; collapse to **4-5-1** (index-10 Al-Hamadi into midfield) if ball enters my final third. Stay narrow to protect the box.
5. **If my player_id ends with "_6" (DM Al-Ammari, #16) and the opposition is breaking past midfield:** Tactical foul — Iraq is happy to take a yellow to stop a counter.
6. **If team_phase == "transition_attack":** Index-10 Al-Hamadi runs the channel; index-8 Qasem carries diagonally; index-5 Bayesh is the trailer; index-7 Zaid Ismael recycles and supports underneath.
7. **If my player_id ends with "_7" (CM Zaid Ismael, #6) and I have the ball in midfield:** Keep it simple — shield, recycle to the index-2/3 CBs or play the nearest forward; do not over-carry. (When Iqbal is on, this slot becomes the creative pivot: dribble forward and seek the through-ball.)
8. **If my role == "DEF" and a cross is incoming into my box:** The index-2 Hashim and index-3 Tahseen attack the ball — both win aerial duels (strength 15 each).
9. **If team is trailing in the final 20 minutes:** Push the index-3 Tahseen forward as an emergency 9 for every set-piece and late cross. Index-1 Doski and index-4 Hussein Ali push high.
10. **If a defensive corner is incoming:** The index-3 Tahseen marks the opposition's most dangerous CF; the index-2 Hashim marks the second; the index-9 Aymen Hussein stays on the halfway line as a counter-outlet.
11. **If my player_id ends with "_1" (LB Doski, #23) and team_phase == "attacking":** Overlap on the left — the index-5 Bayesh cutting inside opens the space.
12. **Set-pieces 20-30 yards from goal:** Defer dead-ball to the index-5 Bayesh (right half-space / central); penalties to the index-9 Aymen Hussein.

## Key Player Notes
- **Aymen Hussein (idx 9):** The target 9. Aerial, hold-up, the team's leading scorer (34 international goals) and primary penalty taker. Scored vs Norway (then unluckily turned in an own goal) before a HT knock — **a genuine fitness doubt for the Senegal finale.** When fit, the focal point of every attack.
- **Al-Hamadi (idx 10):** The Luton Town striker (on loan from Ipswich; first Iraqi to play in the Premier League), 27 international goals. Channel-runner and press-leader off Aymen Hussein; expected to lead the line if Hussein cannot start.
- **Hashim (idx 2) / Tahseen (idx 3):** The aerial CB pair. Strength 15 each. Set-piece weapons in both boxes and the spine of the deep block.
- **Ahmed Qasem (idx 8):** The Eredivisie-based winger who started MD2 at right midfield in place of the injured Ali Jasim — quick, direct (speed 15, dribbling 14), Iraq's likeliest source of a transition spark.
- **Bayesh (idx 5):** The creator from the left and the chief dead-ball deliverer. Late runner into the box.
- **Zaid Ismael (idx 7):** Started both MD1 and MD2 ahead of Iqbal — a hard-running, defensively diligent central midfielder who shields the back four. Functional rather than creative.
- **Bench depth (final 26):** **Zidane Iqbal** (Utrecht, ex-Man United academy) is the headline creative substitute — when Iraq must chase a game he comes on for the _7 slot and becomes the dead-ball taker and press-resistant playmaker. **Ali Jasim** (Como) is the alternative on the right flank. Rebin Sulaka (CB) and Frans Putros are defensive cover; Youssef Amyn adds wide options; Aimar Sher and Kevin Yakob give European-based midfield depth; Ahmed Basil and Fahad Talib back up Jalal Hassan in goal.

## Tournament Mindset
Iraq's first World Cup in 40 years has been a chastening education: a **1-4 loss to Norway** and a **0-3 defeat to France** leave them **bottom of Group I with 0 points and already eliminated** heading into Matchday 3. But the **Senegal v Iraq** finale (26 June, BMO Field, Toronto) is a meeting of two winless, pointless sides — a dead rubber on paper, yet a chance to leave the tournament with pride and a maiden World Cup point. The plan is unchanged: a **compact block, defend the box, frustrate, and counter** through Al-Hamadi, Qasem and (fitness permitting) Aymen Hussein, while hunting a goal from set-pieces. The mentality is **honour the shirt, restore pride, send the country home with something to celebrate** — beat or draw with a vulnerable, equally desperate Senegal, take no silly red cards, and finish the campaign with heads high. A first World Cup point would feel like a victory; a win — over a Senegal side stocked with Mane, Sarr and Gueye — would be a fairytale farewell.
